# Shell fluency ‚Äî from lost at the prompt to querying text like a database üü¢

**Goal.** Find your feet at the shell, learn to program it, wield grep/sed/awk on real text ‚Äî then discover that composing those tools was relational algebra all along, meet the sets underneath it, and finally ask the shell for a *number* and find out it cannot divide 1 by 3. The first three steps are Matt Might's track (he names the survival guide as the prerequisite to bash-by-example); an equivalent Carpentries + Robbins track is a side-quest.

**Audience:** complete beginners welcome; the last two steps ask a little more  
**Effort:** ‚è± half-day+  
**Prerequisites:** none

| # | Lab | You'll learn | Checkpoint (observable) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | [`UNIX_novice_survival_guide`](../UNIX_novice_survival_guide/) | navigate, pipe, redirect, and get help without a cheat-sheet. | in the `~/unix-survival` sandbox, reproduce the guide's `find \| grep READ` and rename `baz`‚Üí`bar`, unaided. |
| 2 | [`shell-intermediate-programming-by-example`](../shell-intermediate-programming-by-example/) | variables, loops, functions, arguments ‚Äî programming the shell, not just typing at it. | run `demo.sh` ‚Üí it prints `5!=120`; then write your own script with a loop + a function that processes argv. |
| 3 | [`UNIX-sculpting-text-regex-grep-sed-awk`](../UNIX-sculpting-text-regex-grep-sed-awk/) | wield the GNU grep/sed/awk trio on real text. | run `demo.sh` (byte-identical on Debian + Alpine); then extract and reformat fields from a log in a single `awk` line. |
| 4 | [`UNIX-relational-algebra-sql-in-the-shell`](../UNIX-relational-algebra-sql-in-the-shell/) üü° | those same tools ARE relational algebra: `cat`=‚à™, `awk`=œÉ, `cut`=œÄ, `comm -23`=‚àí, `join`=‚ãà ‚Äî and Unix never shipped a Cartesian product, so Might writes one in 15 lines of bash. | run `demo.sh` ‚Üí `PASS: all 9 relational identities hold`, i.e. Might's hand-rolled bash, coreutils, and `sqlite3` agree on the same relations; then find why `equijoin -t` silently returns 0 rows. |
| 5 | [`UNIX-set-operations-in-the-shell`](../UNIX-set-operations-in-the-shell/) üü° | the SETS under those relations, and the three algorithm families that build them ‚Äî merge (`comm`), count (`uniq -c`), hash (`awk`) ‚Äî plus why `comm` and `sort -n` silently disagree about what 'sorted' means. | run `demo.sh` ‚Üí `PASS: all 28 set identities hold`; then run `treasure-hunt.sh` ‚Üí it recovers Google's 2008 puzzle answer `7830239` with `uniq -d`, and shows the article's own `comm + sort -n` recipe losing it. |
| 6 | [`UNIX-floating-point-arithmetic-in-bash`](../UNIX-floating-point-arithmetic-in-bash/) üü° | you have mastered these tools on *text* ‚Äî now ask the shell for a *number* and watch `$((1/3))` return **0**, silently. Two people refused to accept that: Cyrus does long division in 12 lines of integer `$(( ))`, and Wood's `shellmath` implements + ‚àí √ó √∑ in pure Bash, dodging the `$( )` fork to beat `bc`. The reframe: it's a *bash* limit, not a *shell* one ‚Äî zsh and ksh93 have had float `$(( ))` all along. | run `demo.sh` ‚Üí `PASS: all 25 checks hold` (bc == awk == pure-bash div == shellmath); then explain why the same run asserts that `0.1+0.2 == 0.3` is **true** for `bc` and **false** for `awk` ‚Äî and why *both* are right. |

### Optional side-quests

- [`shell-novice-workshop`](../shell-novice-workshop/) ‚Äî the Carpentries (CC-BY) full-day equivalent of steps 1‚Äì2, by different authors.
- [`shell-intermediate-workshop`](../shell-intermediate-workshop/) ‚Äî Robbins' 'Bash by example' ‚Äî the Carpentries track's programming follow-on.
